entries.answer: Yes,a radiologist can but the Ethical Rules state: 24. Referral of patients to hospitals.—(1) A practitioner who has a direct or indirect financial interest or shares in a private clinic or hospital shall refer a patient to such clinic or hospital only if a conspicuous notice is displayed in his or her waiting room indicating that he or she has a financial interest or shares in that clinic or hospital and the patient is duly informed about the fact that the practitioner has an interest or shares in the clinic or hospital to which the patient is referred and the patient’s informed written consent is obtained prior to such referral.

entries.question: What is the most common form of practicing in SA. eg Rental of hospital Radiology facility, hospital owns machines and Radiologist rent facility with equipment?
entries.answer: No, the commonest form is that the radiologist rents the space from the hospital and purchases his or her own equipment. This is as everything stands at the moment. We are in a state of flux. The Health Market Inquiry provisional report is due out this month which may propose new regulations. We are also attending a HPCSA workshop next moth where the business practice regulations are being reviewed. Nothing happens very fast so I would use the existing regulations in the short and medium terms.

entries.question: [00090] What is the tariff code and price or nappi code for STERILE TRAY
entries.answer:
Consumables used in radiology procedures: cost price PLUS 26% (up to a maximum of R26,00). (Where applicable, VAT should be added to the above).

1. Sterile trays are billed under the consumable code 00090 00090 Consumables used in radiology procedures 2. Suggest the practice confirm the NAPPI code with the supplier of the sterile tray – there are various suppliers of sterile trays and prices differ (the NAPPI code will either be on the purchase invoice or on the packaging) 3. The practice then needs to do a lookup against the NAPPI price file (MedPrax, Medikredit, etc) they are using in their PMA system (once the NAPPI code has been confirmed as per (2) above.

entries.question: [00110] Is there an imaging code for the Slit Collimated Whole Body Radiography in Trauma (e.g.Lodox) scan currently used in some trauma units. Currently we are using the following codes in public health facilities: 00110 - X-ray skeletal survey under 5 years 00115 - X-ray skeletal survery over 5 years Is the following code valid: 51405 - MRI Cervical spine complete study
entries.answer:
X-ray skeletal survey under five years

The RSSA’s policy on Lodox is: "Slit Collimated Whole Body Radiography in Trauma is out dated, unnecessary technology, which produces whole body-shoot through images in one plane. It will not adequately triage intracranial, spinal, intra-abdominal or severe intra-thoracic trauma. Lodox is not used to any extent in any health system world wide. Trauma throughout the world is dealt with by a combination of conventional digital radiology and the 'gold standard' multislice CT. Multislice CT tomograms will provide the same single plane image as Lodox but in addition provide detailed cross sectional imaging. A modern multislice CT can be installed in trauma units for a similar cost to Slit Collimated Whole Body Radiography in Trauma. The RSSA does not support Slit Collimated Whole Body Radiography in Trauma or similar technology and will not assist in or develop a code for its use.” The code you have suggested is totally inappropriate as Slit Collimated Whole Body Radiography in Trauma is not a MR examination.

entries.question: [00120] Is there a more specific tariff code other than the sonogram code used for "Permcath injection of contrast-? leakage."
entries.answer:
X-ray sinogram any region

The sonar code would be used to assess a fluid collection around the catheter, If contrast was injected: 00120 X-ray sinogram any region would be applicable.

entries.question: What is the appropriate tariff code for checking position of PEG tube (eg. GG study via PEG requested)
entries.answer: As the patient had a PEG he or she presumably couldn’t swallow. No code for specific procedure but gastrograffin swallow or 00120 X-ray sinogram any region could be used as surrogates.

entries.question: What codes should be billed if ultrasound guidance is done in the ward for insertion of a central line by a specialist and what would be the difference in billing if a radiologist is doing the procedure?
entries.answer: In the ward your coding looks correct except that codes 00210 and 00230 would be better than 00250. Our previous recommendation if the radiologist does it all in the ward is: 1/3 reduction in 80640. Mobile ultrasound can be added but mobile fluoroscopy and attendance in theatre not.

entries.question: Can codes 00145 and 60580 be billed together?
entries.answer: 00145 Is the one of the imaging methods for biopsy so it may be added to 80600, 80605 and 80610. It should not be added to the angiography and venography codes.

entries.question: [00150] Can radiographer attendance in theatre be charged together with C-arm in theatre.
entries.answer:
X-ray C-Arm (equipment fee only, not procedure) per half hour

As background – equipment code 00150 - X-ray C-Arm 1. Code 00150 should only be billed by the practice when the practice is the owner of the equipment 2. Code 00150 should be billed per half hour (or part thereof) 3. Should the hospital be the owner of the equipment, the hospital will bill the patient for the use of the C-arm. a. The hospital will most probably use a 4 digit SAMA code to recover their (hospital) c-arm equipment costs. b. Please note that the Radiology 5 digit codes can only be billed by radiologists / nuclear physicians i.e. the hospital cannot use code 00150 to recover the equipment cost Re codes 00155 & 01040 You are entitle to bill these 2 codes together for each half hour (or part thereof) that the radiographer is in attendance in theatre. 1. Please note that you may not add on a code(s) for the region that you are screening e.g. 56120 (pelvis and hips) or code 00135 (control film in theatre). 2. Furthermore – some medical aids will query whether code 00155 (and code 01040) may be charged for the full time spent in theatre or just the actual screening time? a. Code 00155 (code 01040) may be charged for every half hour or part there off, spent in theatre. Radiographers often required to stay in theatre for intermitted short bursts of screening, but she/he must be there when surgeon wants screening, so he/she is tied to the theatre for a significant part of the total theatre time. On the other hand, you cannot bill for the whole theatre procedure time, as some, especially the radiographer owned practices do. Only time actually spent in theatre by the Radiographer may be billed.

entries.question: [00155] Can 00155 and 01040 be charged together.
entries.answer:
X-ray C-arm fluoroscopy in theatre per half hour (procedure only)

You are entitle to bill these 2 codes together for each half hour (or part thereof) that the radiographer is in attendance in theatre. 1. Please note that you may not add on a code(s) for the region that you are screening e.g. 56120 (pelvis and hips) or code 00135 (control film in theatre). 2. Furthermore – some medical aids will query whether code 00155 (and code 01040) may be charged for the full time spent in theatre or just the actual screening time? a. Code 00155 (code 01040) may be charged for every half hour or part there off, spent in theatre. Radiographers often required to stay in theatre for intermitted short bursts of screening, but she/he must be there when surgeon wants screening, so he/she is tied to the theatre for a significant part of the total theatre time. On the other hand, you cannot bill for the whole theatre procedure time, as some, especially the radiographer owned practices do. Only time actually spent in theatre by the Radiographer may be billed.

entries.question: [00160] Could you please clarify the reasoning behind code 00160 (X-Ray fixed theatre installation – equipment fee only) and code 00135 (X-Ray control view in theatre any region).
entries.answer:
X-ray fixed theatre installation (equipment fee only)

00160 – typically in Urology theatre used for retrograde studies. Radiologist must own and maintain the equipment. If such a retrograde study is reported on by a Radiologist code 42155 must be billed for this report. 00135 is meant for views done during a procedure, over and above screening, e.g. view of a prosthesis post insertion or a lateral cervical spine after anterior fusion etc. May only be used if an actual stored image ( film or PACS) if done during an procedure/operation and a report issued by the radiologist.

entries.question: [00210] If a referring doctor in the hospital makes use of radiology's portable ultrasound machine, would code 00210 be the correct code to charge, although this is not a stand alone code and needs to be accompanied by a procedure code.
entries.answer:
Ultrasound with mobile unit in other facility

Suggest you charge mobile in full and 1/3 for part examined. This is the logical converse of rule: ' Where public sector hospital equipment is used for a procedure, the units will be reduced by 33,33%

entries.question: [00230] Is it correct to bill codes 00230 (ultrasound guidance) and 80605 (FNAB) together?
entries.answer:
Ultrasound guidance

00230 is the guidance code and 80605 is the procedure code. Both may and should be billed together. Note the exception for the bundled code 34205 - Ultrasound guided aspiration FNA/localisation of the breast is used for ultrasound guide FNA or marker localisation.

entries.question: Is the following billing correct: FNA: 00230 and 80605 Core biopsy: 00230 and 80610 Hook needle insertion for tumor marking: 34110 and 34205
entries.answer: The general principle is that an imaging modality code plus procedure code is used. The exception to this is the single code: 34205 Ultrasound guided aspiration FNA/localisation of the breast is used for ultrasound guide FNA or marker localisation. There are no codes for either hookwire or marker insertion using imaging method other than ultrasound 80610 is a reasonable surrogate.

entries.question: May code 00230 (Ultrasound Guidance) be charged for if an FNA goes on to be a core biopsy. Are these 2 separate procedures, or if done as a follow on, may the U/S Guidance be charged for again
entries.answer: A guidance code may only be billed once per sitting but a procedure code per nodule or level. In the case of a follow-on, the 34205 should be replaced by 00230 + 80610.

entries.question: What is the correct billing codes for radio frequency ablation in theatre?
entries.answer: The correct billing is 00230 Ultrasound guidance 00210 Mobile ultrasound 01030 Radiological assistance in theatre per half hour If the practice or radiologist has not done a recent ultrasound examination of the area involved it would be appropriate to add an examination of the part eg. 41200.

entries.question: What is the difference between the following codes? 00230, 80605 and 34205
entries.answer: For breast work Fine Needle Aspiration (FNA) is billed as 34205 which is a composite fee for guidance and billing. FNA any other region except breast is billed as 00230 and 80605 Core biopsy of breast or any region is billed 00230 and 80610

entries.question: What are the correct billing codes for a radiologist performing ultrasound in theatre?
entries.answer: The following billing codes are correct. 00230 - ultrasound guidance 00210 - mobile ultrasound 01030 - radiologist assistance in theatre per half hour If the practice or radiologist has not done a recent ultrasound examination of the area involved, it would be appropriate to add an examination of the part eg. 41200.

entries.question: What is the appropriate tariff code for a pleural tap?
entries.answer: 00230 Ultrasound guidance plus either or: 80600 Percutaneous abscess, cyst drainage, any region 82600 Chest drain insertion

entries.question: [00310] What would be the correct billing for CT planning of thoracic spine and pelvis.
entries.answer:
CT planning study for radiotherapy

If done at same time: 00310 plus 53310 If done at two sittings: 00310 and 55310

entries.question: In a case where a CT scan is done for surgical navigation or surgical planning, is there another billing code to be used?
entries.answer: Suggest using 00310 CT planning study for radiotherapy 00450 MR planning study for radiotherapy or surgical procedure with appropriate change in wording of account on 00310/00450

entries.question: [00320] When would the following codes be applicable: 00320 00330
entries.answer:
CT guidance (separate procedure)

These are two codes used for CT guidance There are two different scenarios, the first when the patent has had a diagnostic CT and a decision is made to bring the patient back for a CT guided biopsy at a separate sitting. In this case as the patient has to be scanned again to get positioning and it attracts the more expensive fee 00320. Code 00320 is a comprehensive CT code and implies that the diagnostic study cannot be repeated. The other scenario is when a diagnostic CT scan is done and the biopsy is done immediately without getting the patient off the table. The cheaper code 00330 is used as the patient is on the table already. E.g. For a Chest mass the following combinations would be expected: Different days: 30320 CT of the chest contrasted 00320 CT guidance (separate procedure) Same day: 30320 CT of the chest contrasted 00330 CT guidance (same procedure)

entries.question: It was suggested that 00320 and 81680 should be billed for CT guided botulinium infiltration of the piriformis muscle. Is this correct?
entries.answer: Yes, it is correct.

entries.question: May code 00320 (CT Guidance) be charged “per level” if nerve blocks are done, or is it a “once off” guidance fee for the whole examination (irrespective of how many levels were infiltrated).
entries.answer: Guidance fee – 00320 - to be charged only once per sitting, but the procedure code is billed per level.

entries.question: When is code 00320 used compared to 00330
entries.answer: These are two codes used for CT guidance There are two different scenarios, the first when the patent has had a diagnostic CT and a decision is made to bring the patient back for a CT guided biopsy at a separate sitting. In this case as the patient has to be scanned again to get positioning and it attracts the more expensive fee 00320. The 'comprehensive CT code??? implies that the diagnostic study cannot be repeated. The other scenario is when a diagnostic CT scan is done and the biopsy is done immediately without getting the patient off the table. The cheaper code is used as the patient is on the table already. For a Chest mass the following combinations would be expected: Different days: 30320 CT of the chest contrasted 00320 CT guidance (separate procedure) Same day: 30320 CT of the chest contrasted 00330 CT guidance (same procedure)

entries.question: [00330] When is code 00320 used compared to 00330
entries.answer:
CT guidance, with diagnostic procedure

These are two codes used for CT guidance There are two different scenarios, the first when the patent has had a diagnostic CT and a decision is made to bring the patient back for a CT guided biopsy at a separate sitting. In this case as the patient has to be scanned again to get positioning and it attracts the more expensive fee 00320. The 'comprehensive CT code??? implies that the diagnostic study cannot be repeated. The other scenario is when a diagnostic CT scan is done and the biopsy is done immediately without getting the patient off the table. The cheaper code is used as the patient is on the table already. For a Chest mass the following combinations would be expected: Different days: 30320 CT of the chest contrasted 00320 CT guidance (separate procedure)

entries.question: [00430] What is the appropriate tariff codes for the following procedures: 1. MRI guided breast biopsy with cutting needle 2. MRI guided breast biopsy using Stereotactic Vacuum assist
entries.answer:
MR guidance for needle replacement

MRI guided Breast Biopsy with cutting needle 34410 MR study of the breast pre and post contrast 80610 Cutting needle trochar biopsy any region. 00430 MR Guidance for needle placement + Applicable disposables MRI guided Breast Biopsy using Stereotactic Vacuum assist 34410 MR study of the breast pre and post contrast 34150 X-Ray mammotome hand held biopsy apparatus 00430 MR Guidance for needle placement + Applicable disposables

entries.question: [00510] For codes 00510 – 00560 (Angio tables) – do these codes only comprise of a technical component, or does it include a professional component as well?
entries.answer:
Analogue monoplane screening table

These codes include all components (equipment, staffing, etc) except for the radiologist component (which is included in the 8xxxx procedure fees).

entries.question: [00540] Please can you advise what the appropriate tariff code is for port insertion. Further please confirm that 00540 or 00550 may not be billed in combination whether performed in the providers radiology suite or not.
entries.answer:
Digital monoplane screening table

87653 Percutaneous tunnelled / subcutaneous arterial or venous central or other line insertion No, only one of the machine codes 00510 - 00560 may be used per procedure / patient visit.

entries.question: 1. Are we permitted to bill 00540 with a fluoroscopy procedure as equipment fee? 2. Does the fluoroscopy procedure then include the radiographer and radiologist time, the associated imaging and radiology report?
entries.answer: No, the Rule is: Codes 80600, 80605, 80610, 80620, 80630, 81660, 81680, 82600, 84660, 85640, 85645, 86610, 86615, 86620, 86630, (aspiration / biopsy / ablations etc) may be combined with the relevant guidance codes (fluoroscopy, ultrasound, CT, MR) as previously described. The machine codes 00510, 00520, 00530, 00540, 00550, 00560 may not be combined with these codes. If ultrasound guidance (00230) is used for a procedure which also attracts one of the machine codes (00510, 00520, 00530, 00540, 00550, 00560), it may not be billed for separately. Codes 80640, 80645, 87682, 87683 include fluoroscopy. Machine fees may not be added.

entries.question: [00550] Is it correct for different radiology providers to claim separately in a case where one is the owner of the equipment and the other performed the procedure or should the owner of the equipment perform the procedure?
entries.answer:
Dedicated angiography suite: Digital monoplane unit. Once off charge per patient by owner of equipment.

There would also be no problem if the owner of the equipment (radiologist) billed for the whole procedure and passed on the interventional component to the radiologist doing it. Coversely one radiologist could bill for the machine fee and the second radiologist for the procedure fee.

entries.question: [01030] Will the code 01030 apply if a radiologist assists a doctor with a procedure outside the radiology department (eg. ICU/High Care)
entries.answer:
Radiologist assistance in theatre, per half hour

Code 01030 may be used when a radiologist assists a clinician in an imaging or imaging guided procedure anywhere outside the x-ray department.

entries.question: [01040] Is there a tariff code for Lodox?
entries.answer:
Radiographer attendance in theatre, per half hour

There is no tariff code for Lodox. If a radiographer employed by a radiological practice is used to perform Lodox it is appropriate that the practice should be reimbursed. 01040 is appropriate. We have had repeated queries about whether the fee per part examined may be added to fluoroscopy in theatre, I.e. can for example 65105 be added to the fluoroscopy codes? Our position is that it may be on condition that an image is produced and stored and a written report issued by the radiologist. A similar argument applies to 41110. This may be charged in addition to the fluoroscopy charges when images ( either static or dynamic) are produced and stored and radiologist issues a written report on the cholangiogram. If there are no images and no report the procedure is effectively done by the surgeon and only the fluoroscopy charges apply.

entries.question: If we charge c-arm in theatre, may we still charge radiographer attendance as well.
entries.answer: As background – equipment code 00150 - X-ray C-Arm 1. Code 00150 should only be billed by the practice when the practice is the owner of the equipment 2. Code 00150 should be billed per half hour (or part thereof) 3. Should the hospital be the owner of the equipment, the hospital will bill the patient for the use of the C-arm. a. The hospital will most probably use a 4 digit SAMA code to recover their (hospital) c-arm equipment costs. b. Please note that the Radiology 5 digit codes can only be billed by radiologists / nuclear physicians i.e. the hospital cannot use code 00150 to recover the equipment cost Re codes 00155 & 01040 You are entitle to bill these 2 codes together for each half hour (or part thereof) that the radiographer is in attendance in theatre. 1. Please note that you may not add on a code(s) for the region that you are screening e.g. 56120 (pelvis and hips) or code 00135 (control film in theatre). 2. Furthermore – some medical aids will query whether code 00155 (and code 01040) may be charged for the full time spent in theatre or just the actual screening time? a. Code 00155 (code 01040) may be charged for every half hour or part there off, spent in theatre. Radiographers often required to stay in theatre for intermitted short bursts of screening, but she/he must be there when surgeon wants screening, so he/she is tied to the theatre for a significant part of the total theatre time. On the other hand, you cannot bill for the whole theatre procedure time, as some, especially the radiographer owned practices do. Only time actually spent in theatre by the Radiographer may be billed.

entries.question: [01050] What are the rules concerning the use of tariff code 01050.
entries.answer:
Written report on study done elsewhere, short

Code 01050 is used for the reporting of an x-ray or imaging examination performed elsewhere by another radiologist or radiographer. It may not be charged for simply looking at images performed elsewhere but a written report has to be written. It may not be used for the routine reporting by a radiologist, of examinations performed by a radiographer.

entries.question: [01065] What would be the correct billing for reporting done on X-rays by a radiographer that owns her own practice.
entries.answer: Radiologists and radiographers can work in association either in an employer/employee relationship or as partners in a group practice. Fee splitting is not as issue as the Ethical Rules only prohibit this if the fees are shared with a person who is not registered with the HPCSA or has not taken a part in the service. The latter prevents kick-backs to another professional for referral. Radiologists and radiographers both contribute to the service and fees may be shared. Radiologists and radiographers can form a group practice and bill specialist radiology rates on condition that every case is reported by a radiologist. Radiologists can theoretically bill separately for their services on the same patient. There is a new code: (01065 – Written report for general x-ay studies performed by an independent radiography practice) The principal of using this code has been accepted by Discovery Health which has allocated a benefit to it.

entries.question: [10200] At what age should one start using code 10210 (US of the brain incl Doppler) instead of code 10200 (Neonatal US)
entries.answer:
Ultrasound of the brain – Neonatal

10200 Ultrasound of the brain – Neonatal 10210 Ultrasound of the brain including doppler 10220 Ultrasound of the intracranial vasculature, including B mode, pulse and colour doppler These codes are out of date and in the current review have been bundled int a single code. Until this is published, we would recommend that 10200 is used for the routine neonatal brain and although doppler may be used routinely, it should not be added unless there is a specific clinical indication for it. 00210 Ultrasound with mobile unit in other facility - may be added if appropriate. We would also recommend the use of this code as long as the anterior fontanelle is open and the examination is possible, rather than attempting to stick to a definition of the neonatal period which is ofter difficult to quantify due to prematurity. 10200 Ultrasound of the brain – Neonatal Rule – may be billed for babies under 12 months old 10210 – Ultrasound of the brain including Doppler Rule – may be billed for babies under 12 months old 10220 – Ultrasound of the intracranial vasculature, including B mode, pulse and colour Doppler Rule – no age rule applies to this code I suspect the problem (rejection), comes in when: • The Radiology practice bills for either code 10200 or 10210 for a baby who has not yet been registered with the medical scheme. The account is submitted under the mother’s name, and the system rejects the claim due to the age of the mother. Once the baby is registered with the scheme, the claim can be reprocessed under the baby’s name, and the claim will pay correctly.

entries.question: [10210] At what age should one start using code 10210 (US of the brain incl Doppler) instead of code 10200 (Neonatal US)
entries.answer:
Ultrasound of the brain including doppler

10200 Ultrasound of the brain – Neonatal 10210 Ultrasound of the brain including doppler 10220 Ultrasound of the intracranial vasculature, including B mode, pulse and colour doppler These codes are out of date and in the current review have been bundled int a single code. Until this is published, we would recommend that 10200 is used for the routine neonatal brain and although doppler may be used routinely, it should not be added unless there is a specific clinical indication for it. 00210 Ultrasound with mobile unit in other facility - may be added if appropriate. We would also recommend the use of this code as long as the anterior fontanelle is open and the examination is possible, rather than attempting to stick to a definition of the neonatal period which is ofter difficult to quantify due to prematurity. 10200 Ultrasound of the brain – Neonatal Rule – may be billed for babies under 12 months old 10210 – Ultrasound of the brain including Doppler Rule – may be billed for babies under 12 months old 10220 – Ultrasound of the intracranial vasculature, including B mode, pulse and colour Doppler Rule – no age rule applies to this code I suspect the problem (rejection), comes in when: • The Radiology practice bills for either code 10200 or 10210 for a baby who has not yet been registered with the medical scheme. The account is submitted under the mother’s name, and the system rejects the claim due to the age of the mother. Once the baby is registered with the scheme, the claim can be reprocessed under the baby’s name, and the claim will pay correctly.

entries.question: [10510] Is angiography included in the interventional procedure code?
entries.answer:
Arteriography of intracranial vessels: 3 - 4 vessels

A a general rule the interventional procedure includes the diagnostic angiogram done at the same sitting which is necessary to road map the interventional procedure. If an angiogram is done purely to make the diagnosis and or to assess the feasibility of intervention and is done at a separate sitting that is allowable. Often this is replaced by a CT or MR angio. There should be some flexibility in this approach but the intention is that the diagnostic angiogram should not be repeated at the time of intervention if the indications for intervention are clear and a diagnosis has been made.

entries.question: Is code 13320 only to be used for ENT referral and 13300 for any other provider.

entries.question: [13310] What are the correct billing codes and rates for Cone Beam CT.
entries.answer:
CT of the paranasal sinuses, two planes, limited study

Below please find the (“surrogate”) existing 5 digit tariff codes to be used for Cone Beam CT studies: • 13310 CT of the paranasal sinuses, two planes, limited study - as code equivalent for CBCT sinuses • 16330 CT of the ear structures, limited study - as code equivalent for CBCT ear/temporal bones. • For mandible and implant studies, use the 13310 CT of paranasal sinuses code. Note the wording on the existing 5 digit codes cannot be altered. There is no other appropriately priced CT code available for these studies done on Cone Beam CT (to date there has been no opportunity to introduce new tariffs codes to the radiology procedure list – old “RPL”). Each medical aid will determine their own RCF for the codes above.

entries.question: What is the definition or guideline for a limited sinus study and what would the number of images be.
entries.answer: The problem is that, with modern technology, the scan done for limited and full is the same, a block acquisition. The tariff codes were designed for when each slice had to be set up and done individually. So the difference now is not the number of slices but the scope of the radiologists report combined with the request from the clinician. Complicating this if you give a limited report, for example just on mucosal thickening as an indication of sinusitis, you could be held liable for not reporting other pathology. I can’t give hard and fast rules and I think that this is something that you should discuss with your referring doctors, along the lines of your guidelines, different indications for limited and full as you have suggested and reduced number of images presented for limited. Whether it is 5, 12 or twenty is up to you.

Note the wording on the existing 5 digit codes cannot be altered. There is no other appropriately priced CT code available for these studies done on Cone Beam CT (to date there has been no opportunity to introduce new tariffs codes to the radiology procedure list – old “RPL”). Each medical aid will determine their own RCF for the codes above.

entries.question: [30110] What is the appropriate billing for combined chest and abdominal radiographs?
entries.answer:
X-ray of the chest two views, PA and lateral

As with most these issues this relies on clinical judgement and an assessment of the diagnostic information required. If the problem is predominantly an abdominal one we would support the routine addition of a PA chest with the erect and supine abdomen to exclude perforation or obstruction. We would not support a PA and lateral chest with every erect and supine abdomen. If you clinically suspect either faecal loading or renal calculi a single AP abdomen should be sufficient. In the case where, in addition to abdominal symptoms, the patient has chest symptoms we would support the addition of a PA and lateral chest.

entries.question: Can a PA and lateral chest be charged together with a Barium swallow?
entries.answer: With suspected oesophageal motility disorders, aspiration is an associated problem. PA and lateral Xray indicated for this reason and with any symptoms related to the thoracic oesophagus.

entries.question: [30340] Can 30340 and 30360 be billed together.
entries.answer:
CT of the chest, limited high resolution study

1. Code 30340 (CT limited high resolution) may be combined with 30310 or 30320 or 30330 (CT chest). Motivation may be required. 2. Code 30360, (CT chest for pulmonary embolism) is a complete examination and includes the preceding uncontrasted CT scan of the chest, and may not be combined with 40330 or 40333 (CT abdomen and pelvis). Regarding: Code 30360, (CT chest for pulmonary embolism) is a complete examination and includes the preceding uncontrasted CT scan of the chest, and may not be combined with 40330 or 40333 (CT abdomen and pelvis).

entries.question: Do codes 30360 and 30370 include Pre and Post Contrast studies?
entries.answer: The rule which includes pre-contrast examinations applies to both 30360 and 30370. 30370 is simply 30360 plus venography. Code 30360, (CT chest for pulmonary embolism) is a complete examination and includes the preceding uncontrasted CT scan of the chest, and may not be combined with 40330 or 40333 (CT abdomen and pelvis). Code 30370 (CT pulmonary embolism plus CT venography) may not be combined with 70230 (Doppler).

entries.question: Can code 30360 be combined with any other code e.g. 40337 given than code 30360 is defined as a stand alone code?
entries.answer: As a general rule the answer is "No" but there may be certain circumstances where it is appropriate (with motivation)

entries.question: [33200] What is the difference between a normal Doppler and colour Doppler and are different codes used for adults and children.
entries.answer:
Ultrasound study of the heart, including Doppler

There are basically four types of Doppler: 1. Continuous wave where the sound rather than the image is used. 2. Duplex Doppler, used both to image and assess waveform 3. Colour Doppler which overlays directional colour on to duplex. 4. Power Doppler which is a special form of non-directional colour Doppler. Top end ultrasound machines have had all of the above for twenty years and they are used as needed, by the flick of a switch by the operator.

entries.question: [33300] 1. Is calcium scoring part and parcel/included in a CTCA scan? 2. Is it appropriate for the radiologiy practice to charge for a non-contrasted CT chest as a proxy for the calcium scoring?
entries.answer:
CT anatomical/functional study of the heart

33300 CT Anatomical/Function study of heart for Calcium scoring only 33310 CT Angiography of the heart vessels for full examination including calcium scoring 33300 and 33310 cannot be charged together.

entries.question: When is this code used?
entries.answer: CT Anatomical /Functional study of the heart - calcium scoring only

entries.question: Is it standard practice to stop at calcium scoring only if the the calcium score is 0 and which other clinical findings can be identified when continuing with the full CTCA.
entries.answer: There is a difference in calcium scoring in non symptomatic and symptomatic patients. In non symptomatic patients calcium scoring is used for screening or follow up as a stand alone exam. In symptomatic patients, as in your programme, it is used to assess the feasibility of performing a contrast CTCA. If the score is too high the CTCA is unreliable. If, in a symptomatic patient the calcium score is zero a contrast exam should still be performed as there could still be significant pathology without calcification. Our recommendation is that 33300 should be funded if it is the only exam performed but 33310 should include calcium scoring if done. i.e 33300 and 33310 should not be billed together.

entries.question: What is the alternative tariff code for CT coronary if a patient has a high calcium score.
entries.answer: The two codes are: 33300 CT anatomical/functional study of the heart 33310 CT angiography of heart vessels If a calcium score is done as an isolated procedure either by design or because a CT angio cannot be done due to excess calcium, 33300 should be used. If a coronary angio gram is done 33310 is used with or without a calcium score. 33300 and 33310 should not be combined.

entries.question: [33310] When is this code used?

CT angiography of heart vessels

CT Anatomical /Functional study of the heart - calcium scoring only

entries.question: If ultrasound-guided hookwire localisation is done of both axillae, can both sides be billed?
entries.answer:
Codes 34110 (localization), 34120 (stereo-tactic localization) and 34130 (stereo-tactic biopsy) may not be combined. Code 34130 (stereo-tactic biopsy). Add procedural code 80610 (cutting needle) or 34150 (mammotome) Code 34205 (U/S FNA) includes the procedural code (may not be combined with 34150).

The guiding principle of the five digit coding system is that where possible there should be a single code per procedure. In the case of needle localisation of the breast or axilla there is a single code for this procedure which is 34205 (Ultrasound guided aspiration FNA/localisation of the breast) ; 80605 should not be added to this, there is no aspiration in this case and even if there was that is included in 34205. In the case where this is a bilateral or multiple code 34205 may be used more than once. The correct coding for this procedure is 34205 x 2.

entries.question: [34100] Is there a separate billing code for mammograms for male patients?
entries.answer:

The mammo code 34100 (X-ray mammography including ultrasound ) applies to both females & males. Medical aid should not reject the code because the patient is a male.

entries.answer: There is no additional fee chargeable for tomosynthesis at present.

entries.question: Is there an additional billing code to be charged for a mammogram done after hookwire localisation to check the position of the wire?
entries.answer: Two view mammography is mandatory after mammographic localisation to confirm and document the position of the hook wire. It is however part of the procedure fee for 34110. 34100 cannot be added to this. 34100 should never be billed after ultrasound localisation as ultrasound is used to localise the lesion and confirm the position of the needle post localisation.

entries.question: Please advise if my billing of tariff codes are correct? Biopsy same day as Mammo: 34100 80610 00230 Biopsy on a different day as the mammo: 80610 00230 (or do I add 34205?)
entries.answer: Correct, 34205 should not be added, it is either 80610 and 00230 for core or he combined fee 34205 for FNA, not both.

entries.question: Please can you advise, if the ultrasound included in code 34100, is compulsory or can the ultrasound be done at the discretion of the Radiologist?

entries.question: [34110] Is there an additional billing code to be charged for a mammogram done after hookwire localisation to check the position of the wire.

Two view mammography is mandatory after mammographic localisation to confirm and document the position of the hook wire. It is however part of the procedure fee for 34110. 34100 cannot be added to this. 34100 should never be billed after ultrasound localisation as ultrasound is used to localise the lesion and confirm the position of the needle post localisation.

entries.question: What is the appropriate billing for breast biopsy and localisation.
entries.answer: The rules cover these: Codes 34110 (localization), 34120 (stereo-tactic localization) and 34130 (stereo-tactic biopsy) may not be combined. Code 34130 (stereo-tactic biopsy). Add procedural code 80610 (cutting needle) or 34150 (mammotome) Code 34205 (U/S FNA) includes the procedural code (may not be combined with 34150). The Discovery guidelines are: If the procedure is performed by the radiologist, it should be claimed using codes: o 34150 X-ray mammotome hand held biopsy apparatus AND o 34130 X-ray stereotactic mammography –biopsy OR o 34205 Ultrasound guided aspiration FNA/localization of breast

entries.question: Can 34110/34120 be charged in combination with 34130.
entries.answer: Rule Codes 34110 (localization), 34120 (stereo-tactic localization) and 34130 (stereo-tactic biopsy) may not be combined. Code 34130 (stereo-tactic biopsy). Add procedural code 80610 (cutting needle) or 34150 (mammotome) Code 34205 (U/S FNA) includes the procedural code (may not be combined with 34150). 34110 X-ray mammography study for localisation 34120 X-ray stereotactic mammography – localisation 34130 X-ray stereotactic mammography – biopsy You can only bill one of the appropriate codes.

entries.question: [34120] Is there an additional billing code to be charged for a mammogram done after hookwire localisation to check the position of the wire.

It is however part of the procedure fee for 34110. 34100 cannot be added to this. 34100 should never be billed after ultrasound localisation as ultrasound is used to localise the lesion and confirm the position of the needle post localisation.

entries.question: Is there an additional billing code to be charged for a mammogram done after hookwire localisation to check the position of the wire.
entries.answer: Two view mammography is mandatory after mammographic localisation to confirm and document the position of the hook wire. It is however part of the procedure fee for 34110. 34100 cannot be added to this. 34100 should never be billed after ultrasound localisation as ultrasound is used to localise the lesion and confirm the position of the needle post localisation.

entries.answer: Code 34205 (U/S FNA) includes the procedural code (may not be combined with 34150).

entries.question: Can 34205 and 00230 be billed in combination?
entries.answer: No. Both are ultrasound guidance codes.

entries.question: Can 80605 and 34205 be billed in combination?
entries.answer: No. This is one of the anomalies in the current system, which has been corrected in the new. Suggest the following combination – 80605 + 00230 + Consumables At present FNA of the breast is a stand alone code and should be billed as: 34205 Ultrasound guided aspiration FNA/localisation of the breast Core biopsy of the breast uses the combination of: 00230 Ultrasound guidance 80610 Cutting needle, trochar biopsy, any region FNA any region except breast uses: 00230 Ultrasound guidance 80605 Fine needle aspiration biopsy, any region

entries.question: What are the billing codes for the following procedures: 1. Disposable needle biopsy 2. Semi-auto biopsy gun 3. Vacuum biopsy What is the appropriate billing for breast biopsy and localisation.
entries.answer: These are confusing and have been sorted out in the new version. In their list the first three are disposable items so what is used in charged. Ultrasound guided biopsies are billed: FNA 34205 US Guided FNA (1st lesion) 34205 US Guided FNA (2nd lesion) etc CORE 00230 US Guidance (once) 80610 Cutting needle biopsy (Ist lesion, 2nd lesion) etc plus needle, vacuum gun, semi automatic gun etc In the second list the principle is that a guidance method is used and disposables are added. The rules cover these: Codes 34110 (localization), 34120 (stereo-tactic localization) and 34130 (stereo-tactic biopsy) may not be combined. Code 34130 (stereo-tactic biopsy). Add procedural code 80610 (cutting needle) or 34150 (mammotome) Code 34205 (U/S FNA) includes the procedural code (may not be combined with 34150). The discovery guidelines are: If the procedure is performed by the radiologist, it should be claimed using codes: o 34150 X-ray mammotome hand held biopsy apparatus AND o 34130 X-ray stereotactic mammography –biopsy OR o 34205 Ultrasound guided aspiration FNA/localization of breast

entries.question: [40100] Given the clinical history of abdominal pain only, and no indication from the referring practitioner that they are querying obstruction or perforation, 40100 appears to be the appropriate code. When should 40100 be used and when 40105.

X-ray of the abdomen

entries.question: What are the appropiate views for / protocols correct: 40100 X-ray abdomen 40110 X-ray abdomen multi view inc chest 40105 X-ray abdomen supine, erect, decubites 30100 X-ray Chest 30110 X-ray Chest PA & Lateral 30155 X-ray Chest and Ribs 56100 / 56110 X-ray L/R Hips 56120 X-ray hips and pelvis 55100 X-ray pelvis
entries.answer: Appropriate views should determined by the radiographer and the radiologist. The number of views below is mainly correct but there may be occasions when more or less are required depending on the clinical situation. 40100 X-ray abdomen = 1 x AXR 40110 X-ray abdomen multi view inc chest = 2 x AXR and 2 x CXR 40105 X-ray abdomen supine, erect, decubites = 2 x AXR and 1 x CXR 30100 X-ray Chest = 1 x CXR 30110 X-ray Chest PA & Lateral = 2 x CXR 30155 X-ray Chest and Ribs = 2 x CXR and ribs as required 56100 / 56110 X-ray L/R Hips = 2 x hips 56120 X-ray hips and pelvis = 2 x hips and 2 x pelvis 55100 X-ray pelvis = 2 x Pelvis (AP and Lat)

entries.question: [40300] Is it allowed to charge codes 40333 (CT Abdomen and Pelvis Post contrast) and 30360 (CT Chest for pulmonary embolism) together?

There are times when it is appropriate and we support this combination when appropriately motivated e.g Oncology patient who needs re-staging and has symptoms suggestive of pulmonary embolism

entries.question: Please advise ariff codes for the following procedures:- 1. MR Enterography 2. CT Enterography 3. Loopogram
