entries.question: [40313] What is the correcting coding for multiphasic CT evaluation of renal tract as requested by urologists.
entries.answer:
CT study of the abdomen pre and post contrast

The post contrast studies can usually be done to show two phases with one scan by splitting the contrast injection, the CT IVU. With correct timing you should be able to get a late arterial/venous and excretory phase on one run. So if the patient has not had a pre contrast scan: 40313 CT study of the abdomen pre and post contrast is used and if the patient has had a pre contrast scan as a CT renal colic scan: 40310 CT of the abdomen with contrast.

entries.question: [40333] What is the appropriate study to perform if a CT abdomen ?obstruction is requested.
entries.answer:
CT of the abdomen and pelvis with contrast

40333 is the examination performed by the majority of radiology practices.

entries.question: What would be the appropriate code to use (40333 or 40340) in a patient with a history of abdominal pain.
entries.answer: Under the circumstances with no history of haematuria and no specific requests to exclude pancreatic pathology it is appropriate that a contrasted study is performed. 40340 is not indicated and our recommendation for general abdominal screening for non-specific pain would be: 40333: CT abdomen and pelvis with contrast.

entries.question: What is the appropriate protocol for CT for diverticulitis.
entries.answer: The appropriate codes, for routine use are: 40310 40333

entries.question: [40337] When should 40337 be used instead of 42300 and vice versa.
entries.answer:
CT of the abdomen and pelvis pre and post contrast

All cases of suspected renal colic, with or without haematuria should have non contrast CT, most cases of painless haematuria should have a CT IVU. If all cases with heamaturia are done non contrast the patients are being under-investigated, conversely if all patients with haematuria are investigated pre-and post-contrast with CT IVU they are being over investigated. The referring doctor’s request that all cases should have pre- and post-contract studies is not necessarily appropriate as this does not fit in with international guidelines. We once again state that we cannot support pre and post contrast studies as routine in every case of suspected renal colic.

entries.question: [40340] What would be the appropriate code to use (40333 or 40340) in a patient with a history of abdominal pain.
entries.answer:
CT triphasic study of the liver, abdomen and pelvis pre and post contrast

Under the circumstances with no history of haematuria and no specific requests to exclude pancreatic pathology it is appropriate that a contrasted study is performed. 40340 is not indicated and our recommendation for general abdominal screening for non-specific pain would be: 40333: CT abdomen and pelvis with contrast.

entries.question: When is it appropriate to charge for a triphasic scan (40340).
entries.answer: Triple phase is when the patient is scanned three times post contrast injection. The indications for the triple phase are guideline dependent and not request dependent. The radiologists should assess the need for triple phase bearing in mind current international best practice and the requirement to keep radiation to as low a level as possible. Triple phase is not appropriate for a ‘CT urogram’ as that can usual be done with a phased contact injection and should be charged as a post contrast CT.

entries.question: [40345] Is there a tariff code for a full body CT scan, similar to skeletal surey for multiple myeloma.
entries.answer:
CT of the chest, abdomen and pelvis without contrast

There is no specific code, but 40345 CT of the chest, abdomen and pelvis without contrast could be used.

entries.question: [41130] 1. Please advise if code 41130 (X-Ray transhepatic percutaneous cholangiography) is included in code 84605 (Percutaneous external/internal biliary drainage). 2. Please advise if above codes can be billed for more than once if more than one drainage is done.
entries.answer:
X-ray transhepatic percutaneous cholangiography

The general principle is that a diagnostic procedure should not be billed again at the time of an interventional procedure, however it is unlikely that 41130 will be used as a stand alone procedure so: If 41130 has not been performed 41130 and 84605 may be added. If multiple stents or drains are inserted the second and subsequent drains should be charged at 50% of 84605.

entries.question: [42110] How can the issue of IVP vs. CT (for renal stones) be resolved, keeping in mind that medical aids don't pay for a CT requested by a GP.
entries.answer:
X-ray excretory urogram including tomography

The gold standard for the detection and exclusion of renal stones is non contrast CT. There is no indication for doing an IVU for stones when CT is available. The approach should be; 1. Ensure through CME etc that your supporters are aware of current practice. 2. In the meantime suggest that any potential examination for confirmation or exclusion of stones be discussed with an urologist and the urologist's name, with his or her permission, be used for the referral. 3. We will write to all the medical aids to suggest that those schemes that do not allow direct GP referral and funding of non contrast CT should do so both from a best practice and cost point of view. We will feed back with responses.

entries.question: [42155] 1. Is there an additional code to be charged for a report done on a screening procedure in theatre. 2. Is there a code to be charged for a report on pyelography done in theatre.
entries.answer:
X-ray retrograde/prograde pyelography reporting on images done in theatre

If a written report is made on a fluoroscopic procedure in theatre there is no additional charge for the report. If however during the procedure still images are captured and either printed or stored in a PACS the fee for that part may be levied in addition to the fluoroscopic fee. The fee for reporting is included in this procedure fee. 42155 should be used for reporting a pyelogram done in theatre.

entries.question: What is your recommendation on 42155 being billed although images were not available at time of reporting, but a second formal report, stating the initial date, gets done when images are available.
entries.answer: It is appropiate to bill 42155 if a report is issued on a pyelogram images.

entries.question: [42200] When would be the correct use of codes 42200 and 42210 and can they be used in combination.
entries.answer:
Ultrasound study of the renal tract including bladder

When 42210 is used 42200 is included and should not be added.

entries.question: [42300] In which cases should 42300 vs. 40337 be used? What is to be done if a doctor's standard request is 40337 (pre- and post-contrast)?
entries.answer:
CT of the renal tract for a stone

All cases of suspected renal colic, with or without haematuria should have non contrast CT, most cases of painless haematuria should have a CT IVU. If all cases with heamaturia are done non contrast the patients are being under-investigated, conversely if all patients with haematuria are investigated pre-and post-contrast with CT IVU they are being over investigated. The referring doctor’s request that all cases should have pre- and post-contract studies is not necessarily appropriate as this does not fit in with international guidelines. It is difficult to make a ruling on an individual case such as this. It is our suggestion that you should ask Verirad whether that this is a sporadic incidents or whether your practice profile is out of line with other practices. We once again state that we cannot support pre and post contrast studies as routine in every case of suspected renal colic.

entries.question: What is your advice on IVP vs. CT scan for renal stones due to the fact that GP's cannot request a CT scan.
entries.answer: The gold standard for the detection and exclusion of renal stones is non contrast CT. There is no indication for doing an IVU for stones when CT is available. The approach should be; 1. Ensure through CME etc that your supporters are aware of current practice. 2. In the meantime suggest that any potential examination for confirmation or exclusion of stones be discussed with an urologist and the urologist's name, with his or her permission, be used for the referral. 3. We will write to all the medical aids to suggest that those schemes that do not allow direct GP referral and funding of non contrast CT should do so both from a best practice and cost point of view. We will feed back with responses.

entries.question: [43120] What would be the appropriate billing if a specialist makes use of radiology's screening room to perform a procedure, eg. HSG.
entries.answer:
X-ray hystero-salpingography

If an HSG is done in the radiologist's rooms, the radiologist supplies equipment and radiographer. The radiologist may do the cervical catheterisation and introduce contrast, or this may be done by the gynaecologist. In either case the radiologist will report on the study. One or other of these codes applicable: 43120 X-ray hystero-salpingography (introduction of contrast done by gynae) 43130 X-ray hystero-salpingography with introduction of contrast (introduction of contrast done by radiologist) The same logic applies to other procedures.

entries.question: [43270] What is the correct billing code for a 4D pregnancy scan.
entries.answer:
Ultrasound study of the pregnant uterus, third trimester, first visit

The codes used by specialist radiologists are: 43270 Ultrasound study of the pregnant uterus, third trimester, first visit 43273 Ultrasound study of the pregnant uterus, third trimester, follow-up visit 43277 Ultrasound study of the pregnant uterus, multiple gestation, second or third trimester, first visit There is no additional or unique code for 4D scans and the RSSA would not support one. 4D scans add nothing to the management of the mother or foetus and are usually done for social indications and unsupportable reasons such as 'allowing the family to bond with a 3D image'

entries.question: [50120] Can a lateral view of the spine at the time of the BMD study be billed for seperately?
entries.answer:
X-ray bone densitometry

Some models of Bone densitometry screening equipment are able to produce a lateral view of the spine at the time of the BMD study. This is considered to be part of the study and separate billing for this image as a spine view is not acceptable.

entries.question: [50130] 1. What is the correct billing for fluoro-guided lumbar puncture? 2. What is the ruling on patients being referred by a GP for an interventional procedure.
entries.answer:
X-ray guided lumbar puncture

1. 50130 X-ray guided lumbar puncture plus consumables 2. We have no guidelines on referral of interventional procedures. Interventional cases can be referred to a radiologist for correct indications by anyone. HPCSA rules allow patients to see a specialist without a referral from anyone. The medical schemes are, however , able to make their own rules with respect to benefits, and could require a specialist referral.

entries.question: [52400] Is it appropriate to use code 00410 when codes 52400 and 53400 was done?
entries.answer:
MR of the thoracic spine, limited study

00410 is appropriate if the only sequences were either a single saggital sequence or two saggital sequences, one for each part. If in addition to the saggital sequence one or more axial sequences were done for each segment the 52400 and 53400 codes are appropriate .

entries.question: [53120] What would be standard protocol of views for C-spine and L-spine for "back ache".
entries.answer:
X-ray of the lumbar spine, more than two views

AP and lateral is sufficient for non referred, non traumatic spinal pain in the first instance. There is no indication for routine oblique views.

entries.question: Due to medical aids not paying for lumbar spine and coccyx billed together (53120 or 53110 + 54100), is there an alternative code that can be used.
entries.answer: The common practice is to include sacrum and coccyx, when indicated in 53120. There are very few residual indications for plain film examination of the lumbar spine without trauma. There are virtually no residual indications for plain film examination of the sacrum and coccyx as coccygeal trauma is treated clinically irrespective of an X-ray abnormality. For these reasons we think that 53120 is sufficient and we do not support the bundling of sacrum and coccyx with lumbar spine,

entries.question: [53400] Is it appropriate to charge 00410 if a ltd dorsal spine (52400) and ltd lumbar spine (53400) were done.
entries.answer:
MR of the lumbar spine, limited study

00410 is appropriate if the only sequences were either a single saggital sequence or two saggital sequences, one for each part. If in addition to the saggital sequence one of more axial sequences were done for each segment the 52400 and 5300 codes are appropriate .

entries.question: [54100] Can x-ray of sacrum/coccyx be billed in combination with an X-ray of the pelvis?
entries.answer:
X-ray of the sacrum and coccyx

The codes involved are: 54100 X-ray of the sacrum and coccyx 55100 X-ray of the pelvis The technical answer is yes but it would be in very unusual circumstances and never routinely. Saccral Xray is notoriously poor for detecting sacral fractures. If suspected clinically or on plain film of pelvis, CT or MTR is the examination of choice, There are virtually no residual indications for x-ray of the coccyx.

entries.question: [55310] What codes should be billed if CT planning of thoracic spine and pelvis was requested.
entries.answer:
CT of the bony pelvis complete uncontrasted

If done at same time: 00310 plus 53310 If done at two sittings: 00310 and 55310

entries.question: [56100] Is 56140 a stress study only or does it include the X-ray of the hip.
entries.answer:
X-ray of the left hip

56140 is a stress study only and may be combined with 56100, 56110 or 56120

entries.question: What would be the correct billing if request is for X-rays of both hips.
entries.answer: 56120 X-ray pelvis and hips following general principle that one code should be used when possible. 56100 X-ray of the left hip 56110 X-ray of the right hip Are used when one or other is done.

entries.question: [56110] Is 56140 a stress study only or does it include the X-ray of the hip.
entries.answer:
X-ray of the right hip

56140 is a stress study only and may be combined with 56100, 56110 or 56120

entries.question: [56120] Is 56140 a stress study only or does it include the X-ray of the hip.
entries.answer:
X-ray pelvis and hips

56140 is a stress study only and may be combined with 56100, 56110 or 56120

entries.question: What would be the correct billing code/s if both hips were requested.
entries.answer: 56120 X-ray pelvis and hips following general principle that one code should be used when possible. ------------------------------- 56100 X-ray of the left hip 56110 X-ray of the right hip Are used when one or other is done.

entries.question: [56140] Is 56140 a stress study only or does it include the X-ray of the hip.
entries.answer:
X-ray of the hip/s – stress study

56140 is a stress study only and may be combined with 56100, 56110 or 56120

entries.question: [56400] Do codes 56400, 56410 and 56420 (MRI hip joint/s) include both hip joints.
entries.answer:
MR of the hip joint/s, limited study

As per description, it includes both hips. However if dedicated studies with dedicated coils are done of the individual hips, especially if both are done with intra-articular contrast, then 2 x separate fees are justified. Each hip needs the time of a full study. If both hips are done simultaneously with the body coil only one x billing allowed.

entries.question: [60200] Is it correct that code 60200 may only be charged once per visit, regardless of the fact that different regions were examined.
entries.answer:
Ultrasound upper limb – soft tissue - any region

Once per sitting.

entries.question: [61170] What is the appropriate billing for an MR arthrogram of the shoulder?
entries.answer:
X-ray guidance and introduction of contrast into shoulder joint only

MRI of the right or left shoulder - 61405 or 61400 plus 61170 - guidance and introcduction of contrast into shoulder joint plus consumables

entries.question: [61400] What is the code for MRI arthrogram of the shoulder.
entries.answer:
MR of the left shoulder

MRI of the right or left shoulder - 61405 or 61400 plus 61170 - guidance and introcduction of contrast into shoulder joint plus consumables

entries.question: [61405] What is the code for MRI arthrogram of the shoulder.
entries.answer:
MR of the right shoulder

MRI of the right or left shoulder - 61405 or 61400 plus 61170 - guidance and introcduction of contrast into shoulder joint plus consumables

entries.question: [65120] Can a hand and finger be charged together?
entries.answer:
X-ray of a finger

The rule is that a finger or toe should not be charged if the hand or foot of the same side is done. A right hand and a left finger can be done and vv etc The rules state – “Code 65120 (finger) may not be combined with 65100 or 65105 (hands).” 65100 X-ray of the left hand 65105 X-ray of the right hand 65120 X-ray of a finger

entries.question: [65135] Can a hand (65100/65105) be billed in combination with a wrist (65130/65135).
entries.answer:
X-ray of the right wrist

The rules do not preclude hand and wrist being billed together, when clinically indicated. This should not be done as a routine.

entries.question: [70100] Can 70100 be charged twice if stress views were done of two separate regions?
entries.answer:
X-ray lower limbs - any region- stress studies only

Yes. Two separate studies can be billed. Regions examined should be clearly indicated on report and account.

entries.question: [70200] Are there cases where it is allowed for 70200 to be billed more than once?
entries.answer:
Ultrasound lower limb – soft tissue - any region

70200 Ultrasound lower limb is only allowed once. There are separate codes for ultrasound right knee, left knee, right foot, left ankle etc.

entries.question: Is it correct that code 70200 may only be charged once per visit, regardless of the fact that different regions were examined.
entries.answer: Once per sitting.

entries.question: [70230] What are the indications for billing 70240 rather than 70230 (and vice versa)?
entries.answer:
Ultrasound peripheral venous system lower limbs including pulse and colour doppler for deep vein thrombosis

70230 are used for diagnosis of DVT, usually done with patient supine. 70240 is a more complex study which assesses the patency of the deep system and the competency of the valves between deep and superficial. Needs patient to be standing for part of exam. These are used prior to surgery for varicose veins, when you need to not only identify the superficial veins but make sure the deep are patent.

entries.question: Can 70240 be billed instead of 70230 to compensate for right and left leg?
entries.answer: 70230 is the code for one or both legs for assessment of DVT. 70240 is for examination of the deep and superficial systems to assess varicose veins and the patency of the valves. It should not be used as a routine for the assessment of DVT.

entries.question: Can 70230 and 70240 be charged together.
entries.answer: Codes 70230 and 70240 may never be used together. 70240 Ultrasound peripheral venous system lower limbs including pulse and colour doppler in erect and supine position including all compression and reflux manoeuvres, deep and superficial systems bilaterally 70240 is used for the assessment of incompetence of the venous valves prior to treatment of varicose veins, exclusion of DVT is part of this examination, deep system is mentioned in the descriptor. Conversely 70240 may not be used simply for the exclusion of Deep Vein thrombosis.

entries.question: Should 70230 be charged twice if both legs were done as in the case with arterial Dopplers of both legs?
entries.answer: The fee for venous Doppler, is the same irrespective of whether one or two legs are done.

entries.question: [70240] What are the indication for billing 70240 rather than 70230
entries.answer:
Ultrasound peripheral venous system lower limbs including pulse and colour doppler in erect and supine position including all compression and reflux manoeuvres, deep and superficial systems bilaterally

70230 are used for diagnosis of DVT, usually done with patient supine. 70240 is a more complex study which assesses the patency of the deep system and the competency of the valves between deep and superficial. Needs patient to be standing for part of exam. These are used prior to surgery for varicose veins, when you need to not only identify the superficial veins but make sure the deep are patent.

entries.question: If a bilateral venous Doppler was requested, should 70230 or 70240 be charged.
entries.answer: 70230 is the code for one or both legs for assessment of DVT. 70240 is for examination of the deep and superficial systems to assess varicose veins and the patency of the valves. It should not be used as a routine for the assessment of DVT.

entries.question: What is the correct billing code for a Doppler done for venous insufficiency.
entries.answer: If the practice is examining the lower limbs then use - 70240 Ultrasound peripheral venous system lower limbs including pulse and colour doppler in erect and supine

entries.question: [72110] Some orthopaedic surgeons refer patients to us mainly for probable knee replacements request the following views: 1. Both knees standing (AP AND PA) ? 2 views 2. Single view Lateral of left knee standing 3. Single view lateral of right knee standing 4. Patellar views. Please would advise what your billing recommendation for the above is? We feel that code 72110 & 72120 do not sufficiently cover the procedure performed and that code 72130 should be billed.
entries.answer:
X-ray of the left knee, more than two views

Stress views are not appropriate when the views are taken standing. Standing or supine view is the same thing. For peripheral joints stress indicates active stress with radiologist or mechanical device performing the stress. You should charge for what you do. In this case you are doing AP, lateral and patella views and the appropriate charge would be 72120 and 72125 (Knees including patella).

entries.question: [72120] Some orthopaedic surgeons refer patients to us mainly for probable knee replacements request the following views: 1. Both knees standing (AP AND PA) ? 2 views 2. Single view Lateral of left knee standing 3. Single view lateral of right knee standing 4. Patellar views. Please would advise what your billing recommendation for the above is? We feel that code 72110 & 72120 do not sufficiently cover the procedure performed and that code 72130 should be billed.
entries.answer:
X-ray of the left knee including patella

Stress views are not appropriate when the views are taken standing. Standing or supine view is the same thing. For peripheral joints stress indicates active stress with radiologist or mechanical device performing the stress. You should charge for what you do. In this case you are doing AP, lateral and patella views and the appropriate charge would be 72120 and 72125 (Knees including patella).

entries.question: [72130] Some orthopaedic surgeons refer patients to us mainly for probable knee replacements request the following views: 1. Both knees standing (AP AND PA) ? 2 views 2. Single view Lateral of left knee standing 3. Single view lateral of right knee standing 4. Patellar views. Please would advise what your billing recommendation for the above is? We feel that code 72110 & 72120 do not sufficiently cover the procedure performed and that code 72130 should be billed.
entries.answer:
X-ray of the left knee with stress views

Stress views are not appropriate when the views are taken standing. Standing or supine view is the same thing. For peripheral joints stress indicates active stress with radiologist or mechanical device performing the stress. You should charge for what you do. In this case you are doing AP, lateral and patella views and the appropriate charge would be 72120 and 72125 (Knees including patella).

entries.question: [72405] What would be the appropriate billing where scan gets done by one practice and reports gets done by another practice due to the fact that a radiologist specialised in the specfic field is not available.
entries.answer:
MR of the right knee

It is important to note that the report done by the second practice is not a second opinion and therefor code 01055 cannnot be billed as an initial report was not given in the first place. Stating that the first pracxtice has reviewed the images is not an opinion as neither findings nor conclusion are documented. The first practice is therefore sending the case for a first opinion. Whatever fee is charged by the second practice is an arrangement between the two practices and should not involve either the patient or the medical scheme in any additional cost. The first practice is charging for the study and it is their responsibility to get it reported. Even if the first practice had issued a report we do not support a fee for a second opinion under these circumstances

entries.question: [74120] What are the appropriate tariffs for the following: Left foot Weight bearing views of both feet
entries.answer:
X-ray of the left foot

74120 X-ray of the left foot 74140 X-ray of both feet ? standing ? single view If more than one view standing, it should replace the non-standing view at no additional charge.

entries.question: Is it correct to only charge one calcaneus combined with both feet if both feet and both calcanei was requested.
entries.answer: The rule reads: Codes 74120 and 74125 (foot) may only be combined with 74130 and 74135 (calcaneus) if specifically requested.

entries.question: [74140] What are the appropriate tariffs for the following: Left foot Weight bearing views of both feet
entries.answer:
X-ray of both feet – standing – single view

74120 X-ray of the left foot 74140 X-ray of both feet ? standing ? single view If more than one view standing, it should replace the non-standing view at no additional charge.

entries.question: [74400] Why are there separate codes for MRI ankle and MRI foot but a combined code for CT?
entries.answer:
MR of the left ankle

MR foot and ankle are 2 separate individually honed studies with region specific protocols done with dedicated coils. A CT ankle can readily be extended to include the foot.

entries.question: [80600] What is the correct billing code for insertion of dialysis catheter.
entries.answer:
Percutaneous abscess, cyst drainage, any region

A reasonable equivalent would be; 80600 Percutaneous abscess, cyst drainage, any region 00230 Ultrasound guidance Plus disposables

entries.question: [80605] If ultrasound-guided hookwire localisation is done of both axillae, can both sides be billed?
entries.answer:
Fine needle aspiration biopsy, any region

The guiding principle of the five digit coding system is that where possible there should be a single code per procedure. In the case of needle localisation of the breast or axilla there is a single code for this procedure which is 34205 (Ultrasound guided aspiration FNA/localisation of the breast) ; 80605 should not be added to this, there is no aspiration in this case and even if there was that is included in 34205. In the case where this is a bilateral or multiple code 34205 may be used more than once. The correct coding for this procedure is 34205 x 2.

entries.question: Is it correct to bill codes 00230 (ultrasound guidance) and 80605 (FNAB) together?
entries.answer: 00230 is the guidance code and 80605 is the procedure code. Both may and should be billed together. Note the exception for the bundled code 34205 - Ultrasound guided aspiration FNA/localisation of the breast is used for ultrasound guide FNA or marker localisation.

entries.question: What is the maximum use of the code 80605 during one sitting?
entries.answer: It is recommended that several specimens are obtained in any tissue sampling or biopsy procedure. The diagnostic accuracy increases with up to 4-5 samples. If several passes were made at a lesion, i.e. the needle is inserted through the same region of the skin, this counts as one biopsy procedure. If a biopsy is taken from another site from requiring separate anesthesia of the skin this can be counted as a separate procedure and charged again.

entries.question: [80610] What is the appropriate tariff codes for the following procedures: 1. MRI guided breast biopsy with cutting needle 2. MRI guided breast biopsy using Stereotactic Vacuum assist
entries.answer:
Cutting needle, trochar biopsy, any region

MRI guided Breast Biopsy with cutting needle 34410 MR study of the breast pre and post contrast 80610 Cutting needle trochar biopsy any region. 00430 MR Guidance for needle placement + Applicable disposables MRI guided Breast Biopsy using Stereotactic Vacuum assist 34410 MR study of the breast pre and post contrast 34150 X-Ray mammotome hand held biopsy apparatus 00430 MR Guidance for needle placement + Applicable disposables

entries.question: [80645] Our practice would assist with an ultrasound guided placement of a CVP line should the referring doctor not be able to do so. Can we bill code 80645?
entries.answer:
Peripheral central venous line insertion

The Rule states: Codes 80640, 80645…… does not attract a Machine fees 00510 etc. may not be added, and that 00230 may be added. However, this general rule where machine codes include ultrasound guidance is outdated and no longer applicable. RSSA will support the addition of 00230 to a machine fee when ultrasound is used for guidance

entries.question: [81600] Is there a tariff code for occlusion of more than one aneurysm?
entries.answer:
Intracranial aneurysm occlusion, direct

Following correspondence with Discovery Health and our recent discussion, Discovery Health has agreed to the following: In the existing structure there is a single code: 81600 Intracranial aneurysm occlusion, direct This includes one or more aneurysms. When more than one aneurism are done in one sitting some SANIS members have been re-billing the code for each aneurism . This is not appropriate as patient set up, introduction etc are common irrespective of number of aneurisms done. It is equally unreasonable that you should not be reimbursed when more than on aneurism is done. When more than one aneurysm is done, 50% of the fee should be charged for each additional aneurysm.

entries.question: [81660] What is the correct billing code fo a nerve block of head and neck?
entries.answer:
Nerve block, head and neck, per level

81660 Nerve block, head and neck, per level

entries.question: [85600] Can 85600 be charged twice if done during the same sitting.
entries.answer:
Percutaneous nephrostomy, external drainage

Machine fee once, 85600 may be charged right and left if both done. Should not be used twice for same side.

entries.question: [86610] What is the correct billing for bilateral facet joint blocks.
entries.answer:
Facet joint block per level, uni- or bilateral

86610 Facet joint block per level, uni- or bilateral

entries.question: What is the correct billing for paravertebral joint blocks.
entries.answer: Referring to Paravertebral joint blocks, the codes are: 86610 Facet joint block per level 00145 X-ray fluoroscopy guidance for biopsy, any region If you are doing a spinal nerve block 86610 is replaced by 88615

entries.question: [87653] Can equipment fee be billed with tariff 87653 with regard to the following queries: 1) Does the insertion of permcath require dedicated angiography suite? 2) If the dedicated angio suite is used for this procedure, since the suite is not being used for angio, and only for fluoroscopic guidance can the billing of this be justified?
entries.answer:
Percutaneous tunnelled / subcutaneous arterial or venous central or other line insertion

Percutaneous tunnelled /subcutaneous arterial or venous central line insertion is a complex procedure in the interventional section of the tariff. While this could be done on a high end screening table this would not be optimum and if a dedicated biplane angio suite is available this would be the best way to do it. All the other codes in the range 87600 - 87695 are appropriately done in a dedicated angio suite. The use of the angio suite is related to the interventional nature of the procedure and not necessarily an angiogram. Many of the non angiography interventional procedures are significantly more complex and potentially dangerous compared to a simple angiogram. 00560 is therefore appropriate. Ultrasound guidance was correctly used for initial catheter insertion. This is normal and correct. 00230 and 00560 may be billed together, irrespective of the outdated rule ( equipment code vs ultrasound guidance)

entries.question: What is the appropriatee tariff code for port insertion and can 00540 or 00550 be billed in combination whether performed in providers radiology suite or not.
entries.answer: 87653 Percutaneous tunnelled / subcutaneous arterial or venous central or other line insertion No, only one of the machine codes 00510 - 00560 may be used per procedure: A machine code may be added once per complete procedure / patient visit. This means that any individual code may not be used more than once or more than one code used.

entries.question: [87669] Can 87669 be billed in comination with 00550?
entries.answer:
Embolisation, uterus

Any one may be added to any interventionla code including 87669, but may be billed on its own when radiologist billing uses a hospital cath lab or another radiologists equipment. 87669 should be regarded as the professional fee.

entries.question: What are the rules for billing emergency call out codes 01010 and 01020?
entries.answer:
Code 01010 – Emergency callout:
• Only used when a radiologist is called out TO THE ROOMS to report after normal working hours.
• May NOT be used for routine reporting during extended working hours.
• Charged ONCE per callout for the FIRST modality reported (plain film, CT, US, MR etc.), regardless of the number of examinations in that modality.

Code 01020 – Subsequent cases:
• Used when reporting subsequent cases after having been called out for an initial after-hours procedure. Also used for HOME TELE-RADIOLOGY reporting of an emergency procedure.
• May NOT be used for routine reporting during normal or extended working hours.
• May be charged once per MODALITY for the same patient during the same callout.
• May be charged once per modality for subsequent PATIENTS during the same callout.
• May be charged once per modality for telemedicine reporting.

Definitions:
• Normal working hours: 08:30–17:00 Mon–Fri, 08:30–12:00 Saturday
• Extended hours: routine extensions of these hours in the rooms
• After hours: all hours outside these times and public holidays

Example – In one callout to rooms:
Patient A, X-rays: 01010 × 1
Patient A, CT: 01020 × 1
Patient A, Ultrasound: 01020 × 1
Patient B, CT: 01020 × 1
entries.source_doc: Emergency Call Out Codes 01010 and 01020 (Amended) – March 2013
